coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived nitrogen self-doped carbon dots via a simple one-pot method: Physicochemical, structural, and luminescence properties
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yinghui
Zhu, Chong
Gao, Ying
Yang, Li
Xu, Jiayu
Zhang, Xitong
Lu, Chen
Wang, Yinfeng
Zhu, Yuezhao
coredata.dc:description: The efficient disposal and high-value utilization of biomass are crucial for easing the worsening environmental, resource, and energy situations. “Green” carbon dots (CDs) have got a lot of attention recently and biomass is a good “green” carbon source. In this work, cellulose and protein were used as typical components, and peanut shell, cotton stalk, and soymeal were utilized as typical biomass to synthesize nitrogen self-doped CDs with outstanding optical properties via a one-step and green microwave-assisted hydrothermal method without any additive. The reaction mechanism was briefly analyzed, and cellulose and protein differentiated the functional characteristics of the biomass-based CDs. A close connection was observed between optical properties and physicochemical structure, such as sp2 C, N-doped surface states, and the synergy among them. Pyridinic N, amine N, and sp2 C can reduce the bandgap. Quaternary N is beneficial for anti-interference in the CDs due to the large Stokes shift. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yields were measured as 2.83%–6.81%. The solid state of the CDs from protein, cotton stalk, and soymeal can emit clear cyan and claybank fluorescence under ultraviolet light. CDs prepared from “cellulosic” biomass and “protein-rich” biomass had significant differences in physicochemical, structural and luminescence characteristics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Nitrogen self-doped carbon dots
Microwave-assisted hydrothermal
Physicochemical property
Optical property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the biomass materials and the B-CDs.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of (a) C-CDs, (b) P-CDs, (c) PS-CDs, (d) CS-CDs, and (e) S-CDs.
Nanoparticle diameter distribution of the CS-CDs.
XRD patterns of (a) C-CDs and P-CDs and (b) PS-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs.
Raman spectra of (c) C-CDs and P-CDs and (d) PS-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs.
(a) XPS full-scan survey spectra of the CDs, (b) high-resolution XPS of C1s, (c) high-resolution XPS of N1s, and (d) high-resolution XPS of O1s.
Relative contents of nitrogen from the materials to the CDs.

Images of C-CDs, P-CDs, PS-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs in (a) supernatants, (b) supernatants under UV light, (c) supernatants diluted to sixfold volume, (d) supernatants diluted to sixfold volume under UV light, (e) supernatants diluted to tenfold volume, (f) supernatants diluted to tenfold volume under UV light, (g) supernatants diluted to nearly colorless, (h) supernatants diluted to nearly colorless under UV light, (i) solid state of P-CDs, (j) solid state of P-CDs under UV light, (k) solid state of CS-CDs, (l) solid state of CS-CDs under UV light, (m) solid state of S-CDs, and (n) solid state of S-CDs under UV light.
UV–Vis spectra of (a) C-CDs, P-CDs, and PS-CDs and (b) CS-CDs and S-CDs.
PL emission spectra of (a) C-CDs, (b) P-CDs, (c) PS-CDs, (d) CS-CDs, and (e) S-CDs.
Percentage of nitrogen and its chemical states determined using N1s high-resolution XP spectra and sp2 C determined using C1s high-resolution XP spectra in the CDs.
Optimal emission and excitation wavelengths, absorption peak positions, and Stokes shifts of the CDs.

head.ce:title: Biomass-derived nitrogen self-doped carbon dots via a simple one-pot method: Physicochemical, structural, and luminescence properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The College of Energy, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The College of Energy, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficient disposal and high-value utilization of biomass are crucial for easing the worsening environmental, resource, and energy situations. “Green” carbon dots (CDs) have got a lot of attention recently and biomass is a good “green” carbon source. In this work, cellulose and protein were used as typical components, and peanut shell, cotton stalk, and soymeal were utilized as typical biomass to synthesize nitrogen self-doped CDs with outstanding optical properties via a one-step and green microwave-assisted hydrothermal method without any additive. The reaction mechanism was briefly analyzed, and cellulose and protein differentiated the functional characteristics of the biomass-based CDs. A close connection was observed between optical properties and physicochemical structure, such as sp2 C, N-doped surface states, and the synergy among them. Pyridinic N, amine N, and sp2 C can reduce the bandgap. Quaternary N is beneficial for anti-interference in the CDs due to the large Stokes shift. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yields were measured as 2.83%–6.81%. The solid state of the CDs from protein, cotton stalk, and soymeal can emit clear cyan and claybank fluorescence under ultraviolet light. CDs prepared from “cellulosic” biomass and “protein-rich” biomass had significant differences in physicochemical, structural and luminescence characteristics.

ce:section.ce:para: As a renewable resource, biomass has attracted considerable attention among researchers due to the worsening environmental, resource, and energy situations. The efficient disposal and high-value utilization of biomass are particularly important for the sustainable development of resources and energy and for addressing the environmental problems caused by biomass and its waste. Among thermochemical conversion methods, a hydrothermal technology (HTC) for biomass that is independent of the water content of materials, facile, and environment-friendly is being researched extensively. However, the long residence time and low yield of carbon nanoparticles influence the subsequent utilization of biomass HTC. Microwave-assisted hydrothermal (MHTC) methods have been gradually adopted and investigated by many scholars because of their mild reaction conditions, fast reaction rate, and good product quality. In these studies, the researchers mostly explored and controlled the solid products of hydrothermal treatment as functional carbon materials or fuels but disregarded the products in the aqueous phase. Realizing the high-value utilization of products in the aqueous phase is an important aspect of this field.

In our previous study on the microwave-assisted hydrothermal reaction of different biomass materials, we discovered a type of nanoparticles with an approximate spherical shape and a diameter within 10 nm in the aqueous phase products [1]. The aqueous phase products were preliminarily irradiated using an ultraviolet (UV) lamp and analyzed via UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, fluorescence emission spectroscopy, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The results showed that the carbon nanoparticles in the aqueous phase are a new type of fluorescentcarbondots (CDs) that exhibit good optical properties. CDs have elicited considerable attention among researchers due to their unprecedented and remarkable properties, such as tuned emission of multicolor wavelength, aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy modification, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [2–4]. CDs also have potential applications in bioimaging [5–7], sensors [8,9], theranostics [10], photoelectrocatalysis [10], and light-emitting diodes [11]. In particular, biomass-based fluorescent CDs (B-CDs) synthesized using “green” precursors are “green” CDs [4,12]. Another significant advantage of B-CDs is that biomass may achieve self-doping (the “self-doping” means the doped elements in the carbon dots derive from the biomass itself without adding any other reagents or additional modification.) and complementary enhancement among the different components of the hydrothermal reaction process due to its complicated and diverse components without additional modification and doping.

At present, only a few studies have been conducted on thepreparation of fluorescent CDs from biomass, and most of these studies have focused on the production of high-quality carbon fuel [13,14], the pretreatment of biomass, and the synthesis of high-quality carbon-based materials from solid products through secondary catalysis [15,16]. The aqueous phase products are mostly composed of sugars, phenols, and aldehydes [17–19], which further form CDs through a series of reactions, including decomposition, polymerization, condensation, aromatization, nucleation, and growth [7]. However, the physicochemical structure, surface functionality, optical properties, and luminescence mechanism of the synthesized CDs are also considerably different due to the substantial difference in the components of the aqueous phase products obtained from different biomass materials. Investigating the synthesis of CDs using different biomass materials as precursors is highly significant.

In the present work, five typical biomass-related precursors were used to prepare CDs by using a one-step MHTC method without any additive. Cotton stalk was selected as the typical representative of cellulosic biomass because of its maximum cellulose composition with a content of 48.7% (measured using a Foss Fibertec 2010 cellulose tester). Soymeal, which contains more than 40% crude protein, was designated as a typical representative of protein-rich biomass. Peanut shell was chosen as the representative of generalized biomass. Microcrystalline cellulose and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were used to investigate the effects of typical components on the physicochemical and luminescence characteristics of the B-CDs. Nitrogen self-doping of the B-CDs with good properties was realized, and the differences in the physicochemical properties of the B-CDs were analyzed. The mechanism of and correlation between fluorescence emission and physicochemical characteristics, such as sp2 C and different N-doped chemical states, were examined.

Microcrystalline cellulose was purchased from Wuhan Shenshi Chemical Co., Ltd. BSA (Nanjing Wanqing Chemical Co., Ltd.) was used as the protein precursor. Peanut shell, cotton stalk, and soymeal were obtained from the countryside of Jiangsu, China. The biomass samples were dried for 8 h at 105 °C and crushed by a DLF-18 pulverizer, and then uniformly sieved with a 60 mesh sifter to minimize the impact of particle size on the formation of CDs. Lastly, the prepared samples were stored in a sealed plastic bag and would be dispersed and mixed evenly in water to enhance interaction with the microwave field during the performance of microwaved-assisted hydrothermal treatment (MHTC). Deionized (DI) water was provided by the Smart-Q DI water purification system.

The B-CDs of the five aforementioned materials were synthesized via MHTC. The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Using peanut shell for the representative synthesis, 2 g of peanut shell powder was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon™-lined reaction vessel and fully mixed with 50 mL DI water. A magnetic stir bar was placed in the reaction vessel, and the vessel was sealed and placed in a microwave workstation (maximum power: 1000 W; XO-SM100; Nanjing Xianou, China). Microwave power and magnetic stirring speed were set as 300 W and 200 rpm, respectively. Then, the material was heated to 180 °C and maintained at this temperature for 120 min. After the reaction occurred, the reaction vessel was cooled to room temperature. The obtained product was ultracentrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min to remove the large carbon particles. The centrifugation step was repeated twice. The supernatant solution with the CDs was collected and dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da) for 2 days. A portion of the samples was saved and anotherportion was freeze-dried with a lyophilizer for 60 h for further characterization. Various CDs synthesized from different materials were labeled as follows: C-CDs (cellulose-based CDs), P-CDs (protein-based CDs), PS-CDs (peanut shell-based CDs), CS-CDs (cotton stalk-based CDs), and S-CDs (soymeal-based CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the biomass materials and the B-CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen contents of the biomass materials were measured with a Vario EL Cube elemental analyzer. The morphology of the B-CDs was investigated using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100F). Analyses of X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed to characterize the crystalline structure of the CDs within the 2θ range of 5°–70° (Miniflex 600) with Cu radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). Raman spectra were obtained using a confocal Raman spectrometer (Labram HR800) by irradiating the samples with a 514.5 nm laser. The surface functionalization of the synthesized CDs was investigated via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy experiments on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer using Al Kα X-ray radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV) as the excitation source. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained at room temperature using a TU-1901 spectrometer. Fluorescence measurements of the CDs were performed using a PerkinElmer LS55 spectrometer at various excitation wavelengths. The absolute photoluminescence (PL) quantum yields (QYs) were measured with an FLS980 steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer with a xenon XEL lamp as the light source and a photomultiplier tube sphere as the Eml detector’s light source.

Fig. 2 presented TEM and HRTEM images of the CDs, which showed that C-CDs, P-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs were dispersed well, but several agglomerations occurred in PS-CDs. The PS-CDs was prepared in our phase I experiment. Because of the strong Van der Waals interactions and the long time of storage, PS-CDs tended to stick together and get agglomerated to a certain extent. The long-term stability is still one of the challenges in the field of CDs. The morphology of the CDs was an approximate circle. Based on the TEM results, the particle size ranging from 2 nm to 6 nm of the CDs was counted. Nanoparticle diameter distribution of the CS-CDs was selected as shown in Fig. 3 , because the TEM result of CS-CDs had the largest sample size among the B-CDs, which could better and more scientifically reflect the distribution law of particle size. CDs were selected randomly to measure lattice spacings, and the following lattice spacings occurred in the CDs: approximately 0.24 (C-CDs), 0.21 (P-CDs), 0.31 (PS-CDs), 0.21 (S-CDs), and 0.22 (CS-CDs) nm. Among these values, 0.21–0.24 nm and 0.31 nm corresponded to the (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) facets of graphite, respectively [7,20]. The different lattice spacings implied the random orientation of the B-CDs [21].

The XRD pattern illustrates the crystal structure of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 4 , all of which present broad diffractions, which are indicative of poor crystallinity [22]. The broad 2θ patterns at around 23° and 42° of PS-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs (Fig. 4b) can be assigned to the high degree (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes of graphite because of the disordered carbon atoms [3,7,23]. These findings are consistent with the TEM results. C-CDs and P-CDs also displayed broad peaks but at less than 2θ around 20° (Fig. 4a), further indicating the existence of an amorphous structure [24], with peaks rarely appearing at 42°.

Fig. 5 displays the Raman spectra of all the CDs, which showed a D band around 1382–1398 cm−1 and a stronger G band around 1544–1569 cm−1, corresponding to the sp3 and sp2 defects in the CDs, respectively [3,7,25]. The relative intensity ratio of the two peaks was approximately 0.95, indicating the low graphitization degree and the presence of many defective carbon structures in the CDs [26], probably due to the successful doping of nitrogen atoms and oxygen-rich functional groups in the CDs.

The FTIR spectra are shown in Fig. 6 . The absorption bands of PS-CDs and CS-CDs were similar to those of C-CDs, and those of S-CDs were similar to those of P-CDs. These findings indicate that cellulose and protein, as important components of biomass, directly affect the functional characteristics of the CDs. Protein exerts an important influence on the formation of functionality in carbon-based materials [27]. Cellulose, whose content in biomass is nearly 40%–50%, contributes to the formation of a carbonaceousframework [27]. A broad band around 3400 cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibration of hydrogen bonded OH in C-CDs, PS-CDs, and CS-CDs [19,20,28]. The polar functional groups on the surface resulted in highhydrophilia and good dispersion in water for the as-synthesized CDs [22]. It was found that when the freeze-dried CDs were exposed to air, the C-CDs, PS-CDs and CS-CDs would absorb moisture quickly, while the P-CDs and S-CDs could maintain a loose powder form as shown Fig. 9(i), (m). Therefore, the P-CDs and S-CDs might have hydrophobicity to some extent. The raw materials of P-CDs and S-CDs both contain protein. Hydrophobic interactions are the main drivers of protein folding. Hydrophobic residues in the CDs synthesized from protein-containing materials played a role in the hydrophobicity of CDs. Hydrophilic groups, such as OH and COOH vibrations, were extremely weak and nearly disappeared in P-CDs and S-CDs; therefore, apart from the hydrophobicresidues in CDs, they also contributed to the hydrophobic properties of P-CDs and S-CDs [22]. Another peak was observed around 3270 cm−1 in P-CDs and S-CDs due to the stretching vibrations of NH [7,23,25]. Compared with that in C-CDs, the additional peak of CN stretching vibration around 1279 cm−1 in PS-CDs suggested the successful doping of nitrogen atoms in PS-CDs but was weaker in CS-CDs. The amide I (1660 cm−1) and amide II (1530 cm−1) bands were two major bands of the protein FTIR spectrum [29]. In the P-CD infrared spectrum, amide II disappeared but amide I remained. These results indicated that the structure of protein was destroyed but the dehydration and carbonization of proteins were incomplete. The sharp peaks in the 1603–1667 cm−1 region might also correspond to the absorption of CC and COOH vibrations [3,20,22,25,30]. The characteristic absorption bands at 1454 cm−1 were assigned to amide III of CN and stretched in S-CDs and P-CDs. These features further illustrated the successful self-doping of nitrogen atoms in the B-CDs. In addition, the bands at 2923–2964, 1398–1400, and 1032–1102 cm−1 in all the CDs were attributed to CH stretching vibration, CH deformation stretching [19], and CO stretching [3,24,25], respectively. Notably, the OH (3400 cm−1) and CO (1032 cm−1) band intensity of C-CDs > CS-CDs > PS-CDs might be attributed to the cellulose content of the three materials microcrystalline cellulose (100%) > cotton stalk (48.7%) > peanut shell (36.7%). In the process of MHTC, cellulose underwent dehydration and pyran ring-opening reaction to form small molecules, such as hydroxyacetaldehyde and 5-(hydroxymethyl)furfural, which was closely related to the OH and CO. The content of cellulose affected the content of OH and CO, which was reflected in the band intensity of functional groups. The small bands around 1715 cm−1 could be overlapped by CC, and COOH vibration was assigned to the CO group [7,22,30]. The aforementioned FTIR results confirmed the introduction of abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups into the B-CDs via MHTC.

The chemical structure and states of elements in the CDs were analyzed using the XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 7 . The XPS full-scan spectra of the CDs (Fig. 7a) show the existence of carbon (C1s, 284.9 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 398.9 eV), and oxygen (O1s, 530.9 eV) in P-CDs, PS-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs, and only carbon and oxygen in C-CDs. The C1s spectrum of C-CDs (Fig. 7b) presents four peaks at 284.5 (CC [sp2]), 285 (CC [sp3]), 286.3 (CO), and 287.9 (CO) eV. Meanwhile, the other CDs exhibit five main peaks at 284.5, 284.8–285, 286–286.1, 287.3, and 288.1 eV, corresponding to CC (sp2), CC (sp3), CN, CO, and CO, respectively [7,23]. Apart from C-CDs, the N1s spectra are fitted by four peaks (Fig. 7c), which are attributed to pyridinic N (398.5 eV), amine N (399.7 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV), and quaternary N (401.3 eV) [31]. The spectra of O1s (Fig. 7d) were deconvoluted to two peaks at 530.6–531.6 eV and 532.4–532.7 eV, which were related to the presence of CO and COH/COC, respectively. The relative atomic percentages of nitrogen in the synthesized CDs were 14.1% (P-CDs), 3.6% (PS-CDs), 2.3% (CS-CDs), and 7.2% (S-CDs), further demonstrating the FTIR results and the doping of nitrogen in the CDs. The atomic percentages of nitrogen in the self-doped P-CDs and S-CDs were considerable [25]. Nitrogen in the different materials completed varying proportions of N-doping. The relative contents of nitrogen in the materials and synthesized CDs were normalized as shown in Fig. 8
. The proportion of N-doping with peanut shell as the precursor was clearly higher than that with cotton stalk as the precursor when the relative nitrogen contents of these materials were nearly equal. This finding indicates that other constituents in the biomass also affect N-doping. Comparing soymeal with the two other biomass materials, the gap among relative nitrogen contents narrowed from the materials to the synthesized CDs, suggesting that a large amount of nitrogen in soymeal was not converted completely.

The high-resolution XPS N1s peaks of the CDs presented different relative fractions of pyridinic N, amine N, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N (Table 1 ). S-CDs had the highest fraction of pyridinic N, CS-CDs of quaternary N, and P-CDs of amine N and pyrrolic N. Moreover, the relative fractions of CC (sp2) in the high-resolution XPS C1s are provided in Table 1. S-CDs presented the highest CC (sp2) fraction. The sp2 domain dominated the PL emission of the intrinsic state (i.e., the carbon core state) and the previous report illustrated it made a few CDs present quantum size effect to a certain extent [32]. However, the confirmation of the carbon core state is always indirect, and the effect of the carbon core state can be hidden by another strong PL center, such as the different surface states of nitrogen. The complex composition of a biomass precursor exerted varying effects on the chemical state of the doping nitrogen. This phenomenon would have a crucial effect on the optical properties of the nitrogen-doped B-CDs.

By FTIR analysis, cellulose and protein differentiated the functional characteristics of the B-CDs. Based on the Ref. [27] and the above FTIR and XPS analysis, cellulose and protein, as typical components of biomass, exerted important influences on the formation of the carbonaceousframework and functionality in carbon-based materials. In the hydrothermal conversion of cellulose, hydrogen and glycosidic bonds broke successively. Intermediate products with a large amount of oligomeric-reducing sugars, such as levoglucosan, were formed. Reducing sugars and cellulose also underwent dehydration and pyran ring-opening reaction to form small molecules, such as hydroxyacetaldehyde and 5-(hydroxymethyl)furfural. Apart from water, abundant polar molecules in the intermediates that were capable of absorbing microwave radiation heated the intermediates rapidly and uniformly, resulting in homogeneous materials with fast production. Cross-linking and condensation followed for the van der Waals force and hydrogen bonds among the polysaccharide and small molecules [1]. Ultimately, hydrothermal carbonization was completed to produce CDs with more condensed and compact aggregates. From the FTIR and XPS results, sp2 C and many CO and CO functional groups were formed in C-CDs. sp2 C may be related to the intrinsic luminescence, and CO and CO may be related to the surface luminescence of the CDs [33]. Proteins were hydrolyzed into amino acids. The subsequent major reactions of decarboxylation and deamination occurred in amino acids to produce amines and low-molecular-weight organic acids, respectively. Decarboxylation is more likely to occur than deamination, followed by cross-linking and condensation. Simultaneously, the self-doping of nitrogen was completed for a special self-polymerization using the nucleophilic reaction of arc electrons on nitrogen [10]. As shown by the XPS results, four chemical states of nitrogen were formed in P-CDs. The CDs prepared from cellulose and protein via MHTC demonstrated different physicochemical properties, which exerted important effects on the luminescence characteristics.

Agricultural and forestry wastes, such as peanut shells, cotton stalks, and soymeal, are primarily composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, and protein. During MHTC, cellulose and hemicellulose in biomass were first decomposed into intermediate states composed of C/H/O elements, such as oligomeric-reducing sugars, through dehydration and chain breaking [10]. However, lignin barely decomposed during the hydrothermal process due to the low reaction temperature. Moreover, the hemicellulose content of biomass was low. Therefore, the formation mechanism of the CDs from biomass through MHTC can be briefly understood from the interaction between cellulose and protein. The interaction among the major components of biomass can be primarily attributed to the Maillard reaction among their hydrolysis products, oligomeric-reducing sugar, and amino acids. A variety of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing heterocyclic compounds were formed through the Maillard reaction. From the preceding analysis of the physicochemical characteristics, these nitrogen- and oxygen-containing heterocyclic compounds and other intermediate products also underwent polymerization, cross-linking, and carbonization to form the B-CDs. The carbon core and different nitrogen-doped states of the B-CDs exerted important effects on luminescence characteristics, as analyzed in detail in the subsequent section.

The synthesized CDs in the supernatant solutions are shown in Fig. 9(a). The color of the supernatant solutions may indicate the different carbonization degrees and concentrations of various CDs under the same experimental conditions [24]. Fig. 9(b) shows the image of the supernatant solutions under UV light with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. All the supernatants emitted cyan PL, and the inner filter effect of fluorescence could be observed in the P-CD, PS-CD, CS-CD, and S-CD supernatants for the high-concentration CDs. These supernatants were diluted to sixfold (Fig. 9(c) and (d)) and tenfold (Fig. 9(e) and (f)) volumes. The inner filter effect of the four latter types of supernatants was weakened, but the luminescence effect was enhanced. The supernatants were further diluted to become nearly colorless (Fig. 9(g) and (h)). With the exception of the C-CD supernatant, the others still exhibited evident luminescence effects, particularly the P-CD and S-CD supernatants. The absolute PL QYs of the solution samples were measured at 6.20%, 6.81%, 2.83%, 2.95% and 4.92% for C-CDs, P-CDs, PS-CD, CS-CDs, and S-CDs, respectively; these values are close to the results of other reported B-CDs [34], and higher than those in several studies [4,35]. The results were considerable for the one-step and green economic syntheses when using biomass as the precursor without any modification. Unexpectedly, the solid states of P-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs after lyophilization can also emit clear cyan and claybank fluorescence when induced byUVlight, as shown in Fig. 9(i)–(n). The solid states of P-CDs and S-CDs experienced difficulty in absorbing moisture from air. In accordance with the FTIR analysis, this result could be attributed to the hydrophobic characteristics of P-CDs and S-CDs.

Furthermore, the optical properties of the CDs were explored through UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopy, as shown in Figs. 10 and 11 , respectively. Two characteristic shoulder peaks were located at 273 nm and 322 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of P-CDs. All the other CDs presented one absorption peak similar to the Gaussian peak at 281 (C-CDs), 278 (PS-CDs), 299 (CS-CDs), and 328 nm (S-CDs). These characteristic Gaussian peaks were ascribed to the fluorophores on the surface of the CDs, as previously reported [32]. Moreover, carbon cores could be formed and eventually affected system fluorescence at the expense of the amide-containing molecular fluorophores. The peaks at <300 nm were attributed to the π–π* transition of the conjugated CC that corresponded to the carbon core. However, another study indicated that the peaks at 322 nm and 328 nm assigned to the n–π* transition probably corresponded to the effect of nonbonding orbitals from pyridinic N and pyrrolic N [31]. The absorption peak intensity of PS-CDs is considerably higher than that of C-CDs. This finding suggests that the self-passivation and self-doping of complex components during PS-CD synthesis may strengthen fluorescence properties. Self-doping can produce additional surface defects to promote electron transfer and radiative recombination. Combined with the XPS results (Table 1), the impact of different N-doped chemical states on the absorption spectra was analyzed. The absorption peak intensity of PS-CDs was stronger than the first peak (273 nm) of P-CDs, probably due to pyridinic N and quaternary N. Meanwhile, that of S-CDs was considerably stronger than that of CS-CDs. This finding further demonstrated that pyridinic N plays an important role in the absorption properties of CDs. The peak of S-CDs shifted to 328 nm compared with that of CS-CDs (299 nm). The redshift from π–π* to n–π* transition was likely ascribed to the reduction of the bandgap produced by pyridinic N and amine N. This finding is consistent with that of a previous research [31]. The second absorption peak (322 nm) intensity of P-CDs was higher than that of PS-CDs, probably due to amine N and pyrrolic N. This result further confirmed that amine N can reduce the bandgap and n–π* transition that correspond to the effect of nonbonding orbitals from pyrrolic N. Notably, the order of absorption intensity among C-CDs, P-CDs, and PS-CDs is consistent with the amount of CC (sp2) (the sp2 C contents of PS-CDs > P-CDs > C-CDs). Similarly, the stronger absorption intensity of S-CDs corresponds to its higher sp2 C content compared with that of CS-CDs. Moreover, sp2 C can also act as a building block that links oxygen- or nitrogen-containing groups and possibly dominates the luminescence of the intrinsic state [10]. The different chemical states show their individual impact on the absorption spectra. However, the luminescence of the B-CDs may not only be attributed to sp2 C or different N-doped surface states but also to the synergy among them.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs (Fig. 11) were measured using the excitation wavelength from 290 nm to 380 nm in 10 nm increments for C-CDs, P-CDs, and PS-CDs and from 280 nm to 500 nm in 20 nm increments for CS-CDs and S-CDs. The CDs clearly demonstrated an excitation-dependent emission character. The emission peak positions of the CDs shifted to longer wavelengths with an increase in the excitation wavelength. The redshift of PL emissions could be attributed to the coexistence of CDs with different sizes [29]. The excitation wavelengths (EX-ws) and emission wavelengths (EM-ws) of the maximum PL intensity for the CDs are listed in Table 2 . A redshift was observed among EX-ws following the order of C-CDs, P-CDs, PS-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs. The order for EM-ws was C-CDs < PS-CDs < P-CDs < CS-CDs < S-CDs, indicating that the bandgap possibly decreased for the sp2 C and N-doped surface states, particularly amine N [29], quaternary N [31], and pyridinic N. These findings further demonstrated the UV–Vis absorption results, i.e., the PL properties may be closely related to sp2 C, different N-doped surface states, and the synergy among them. The Stokes shifts were calculated as the energy difference between the maximum absorption and the emission maximum [36], as shown in Table 2. The Stokes shifts of the CDs from biomass were larger than those from a single component. This result corresponds to the complementary and reinforcement actions of the cellulose and protein in biomass during the self-passivation and self-doping processes. The overlapping of the excitation and emission peaks can be effectively avoided with large Stokes shifts to reduce self-absorption and improve anti-interference; these features indicate that the B-CDs have high potential in the field of fluorescent probe application [37]. Notably, the largest Stokes shift was achieved by CS-CDs, probably indicating that quaternary N is beneficial for the improvement of anti-interference in fluorescent carbon. All the aforementioned results demonstrated that the highly fluorescent B-CDs were successfully synthesized via one-step MHTC and exhibited excellent optical properties without doping or modification with any chemical reagents.

In this work, biomass was divided into “cellulosic”, “protein-rich” and “generalized” biomass for the first time. Nitrogen self-doped CDs with good properties were synthesized from different biomass materials using a simple and green MHTC method without any additive. The solid states of P-CDs, CS-CDs, and S-CDs could emit evident cyan and claybank fluorescence under UV light. The CDs from cellulosic- and protein-rich biomass demonstrated two types of functional characteristics with significant differences, and their formation mechanism could be briefly understood through the interaction between cellulose and protein. The optical properties were closely related to sp2 C, N-doped surface states, and the synergy among them. Pyridinic N, amine N, and CC (sp2 C) could reduce the bandgap. Quaternary N might be beneficial for anti-interference in CDs. This work can provide a research basis for the preparation of CDs from “cellulosic”, “protein-rich” biomass and their synergistic reactions to realize the efficient disposal and high-value utilization of biomass. Next, we will continue to explore the optimization of the performance of B-CDs, such as quantum yield, optical property and stability. The “green” CDs from biomass with good properties will have great potential applications in relevant fields.

Yinghui Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Chong Zhu: Investigation, Validation. Ying Gao: Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Li Yang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jiayu Xu: Investigation. Xitong Zhang: Investigation. Chen Lu: Investigation. Yinfeng Wang: Project administration. Yuezhao Zhu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their appreciation for the financial support provided by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (18KJB480005), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51406063 and 51676096), the Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Process Enhancement and New Energy Equipment Technology, and the Top-notch Academic Program Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.
